questions.question_text: What is the standard redemption period after a judicial foreclosure sale in Montana?
questions.explanation: Montana provides a one-year statutory redemption period after a judicial foreclosure sale. During this period, the borrower can redeem the property by paying the sale price plus interest and costs.

questions.question_text: To redeem after judicial foreclosure, a borrower must pay what amount?
questions.correct_answer: Sale price plus interest and costs
questions.explanation: To exercise the statutory right of redemption after judicial foreclosure in Montana, the borrower must pay the foreclosure sale price plus accrued interest and associated costs within the one-year redemption period.

questions.question_text: A judicially foreclosed Montana property sits vacant and appears abandoned after the sheriff's sale. What happens to the borrower's redemption period?
questions.explanation: Montana law contains no abandonment exception. Under MCA 25-13-802, the judgment debtor or a redemptioner may redeem 'any time within 1 year after the sale' of a judicially foreclosed property, whether or not it is occupied. The only 60-day rule in Montana redemption law is unrelated: MCA 25-13-803 lets a subsequent redemptioner re-redeem within 60 days after the last redemption. Some states do shorten redemption for abandoned property, but Montana has no such statute.

questions.question_text: A bank chooses judicial foreclosure instead of trustee sale. What is the primary advantage for the borrower?
questions.explanation: The primary advantage of judicial foreclosure for the borrower is the one-year statutory redemption period after the sale. In contrast, a non-judicial trust deed foreclosure provides no post-sale redemption right.

questions.question_text: Judicial foreclosure in Montana requires proceedings in which forum?
questions.explanation: Judicial foreclosure in Montana requires the lender to file a lawsuit in district court. The court supervises the process, orders the sale, and the borrower receives the one-year statutory redemption period afterward.

questions.question_text: Which security instrument allows non-judicial foreclosure in Montana?
questions.explanation: Deeds of trust are commonly used in Montana real estate financing. They involve three parties (borrower, lender, trustee) and include a power of sale clause allowing non-judicial foreclosure through the trustee.

questions.question_text: In a trust deed foreclosure, what is the minimum notice period before the sale?
questions.explanation: Montana requires the recorded notice of trustee's sale to be mailed to the borrower at least 120 days before the date fixed for the sale (MCA 71-1-315). This gives the borrower time to cure the default or arrange alternative financing.

questions.question_text: After a trustee's sale under a deed of trust, does the borrower have a redemption period?
questions.explanation: Unlike judicial foreclosure, there is no statutory right of redemption after a non-judicial trust deed foreclosure sale in Montana. The trustee's sale is final, making this a key distinction between the two foreclosure methods.

questions.question_text: A borrower defaults on a 10-acre property with a deed of trust. Which expedited process may apply?
questions.explanation: Montana's Small Tract Financing Act (MCA 71-1-301 et seq.) authorizes trust indentures — and their streamlined non-judicial trustee's sale — for real property of not more than 40 acres (MCA 71-1-304). This 10-acre property qualifies. The limit was originally 3 acres in 1963 and has been raised by amendment to the current 40 acres.

questions.question_text: A homeowner facing non-judicial foreclosure wants to stop the sale. When is the last chance to cure?
distractors: There is no right to cure
questions.explanation: In a Montana non-judicial foreclosure, the borrower can cure the default and stop the sale at any time before the trustee's sale occurs. Once the sale is completed, there is no redemption period for trust deed foreclosures.

questions.question_text: Under Montana's Statute of Frauds, which contract does NOT need to be in writing?
questions.explanation: Montana's Statute of Frauds requires writing for real estate contracts including purchase agreements, listing agreements, deeds, and leases over one year. A 6-month lease falls under one year and does not require writing.

questions.question_text: Which element is required in a Montana real estate contract under the Statute of Frauds?
questions.explanation: Montana's Statute of Frauds requires real estate contracts to contain the parties' names, property description, price or consideration, and signatures — strictly, the signature of the party to be charged or that party's agent (MCA 28-2-903). Notarization, witnesses, and attorney approval are not required for validity.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller shake hands, and the buyer moves in and makes improvements. Can the buyer enforce this oral deal?
distractors: No, oral deals are never valid
Only if a broker witnessed it
questions.explanation: While oral real estate contracts generally violate the Statute of Frauds, the part performance doctrine may apply when one party has substantially performed (taking possession, making improvements, paying consideration). Courts may enforce to prevent injustice.

questions.question_text: A deed conveying property must be in writing because of which legal requirement?
questions.explanation: The Statute of Frauds requires all deeds conveying real property to be in writing. This applies to all conveyance instruments in Montana, including warranty deeds, quitclaim deeds, and trust deeds.

questions.question_text: Which of the following must be in writing under Montana's Statute of Frauds?
distractors: A verbal promise to mow a lawn
questions.explanation: Montana's Statute of Frauds requires mortgages and trust deeds on real property to be in writing. Short-term rentals under one year, utility agreements, and service promises do not fall under the statute's writing requirement.
